name: King Oliver
description: American jazz cornet player and bandleader
abstract: Joseph Nathan "King" Oliver (December 19, 1881 – c. April 10, 1938) was an American jazz cornet player and bandleader.

about_quotes.speaker: Jim Cullum
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee discusses King Oliver (Joe Oliver) as a historical figure he would like to have seen, and mentions doing a tribute show to him. The interviewer asks about 'Joe' (referring to the guest cornet player hired for the tribute, not King Oliver), and the interviewee describes working with t
about_quotes.doc_title: Jim Cullum interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Bob Havens
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewer asks if the music was similar to King Oliver's and Louis Armstrong's, and the interviewee responds by discussing the history of Dixieland and ragtime generally, without indicating a personal connection to King Oliver.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bob Havens interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Peter Vacher
about_quotes.evidence: I think the focus then, and probably has continued to be, it was on the great black innovators. That sense that the momentum of the music came from these key people, from Jelly Roll, from Louis, from King Oliver if you like.
about_quotes.doc_title: Peter Vacher interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 18, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Muranyi
about_quotes.evidence: I am an old revivalist. You know after World War II there were the moldy figs, the ones that were interested in old records and King Oliver and Jelly Roll Morton and that kind of stuff, you know going back to the original.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Muranyi interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 11, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Dan Morgenstern
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee discusses King Oliver in the context of historical records, correspondence from the 1930s, and Oliver's career decline, but never claims a personal interaction or relationship with him.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dan Morgenstern interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 9, 2013

about_quotes.speaker: Al Vollmer
about_quotes.evidence: Clyde had some blues with King Oliver and played with him in the 30s... Clyde Bernhardt represents almost a microcosm in the history of jazz, having played with King Oliver and Charlie Parker.
about_quotes.doc_title: Al Vollmer interviewed by Monk Rowe, New Hartford, New York, June 8, 2017
